symptoms: Orange-brown circular pustules on upper leaf surface; yellow halos around pustules; leaves yellow and dry prematurely; pustules turn dark brown late season
immediate_actions: Remove and destroy heavily infected leaves; improve airflow between plants; stop overhead irrigation; apply foliar fungicide if more than 5% leaf area affected
preventive_measures: Plant resistant varieties; rotate crops every 2-3 years; plant early to avoid peak spore periods; balanced nitrogen
chemical_controls: Propiconazole (Tilt), Tebuconazole (Folicur), Azoxystrobin (Amistar), Trifloxystrobin+Propiconazole (Stratego)
organic_controls: Sulfur dust or wettable sulfur; neem oil spray; potassium bicarbonate solution; copper-based fungicides
seasonal_risk: Spring through early summer; highest risk at 15-22C with leaf wetness over 6 hours
spread_mechanism: Airborne urediniospores; wind dispersal over long distances; spreads rapidly in humid conditions
notes: Act within 7-10 days of first symptoms; late treatment rarely saves yield; monitor weekly during heading stage

symptoms: Brick-red elongated pustules on stems and leaf sheaths; stems may lodge or break; pustules turn black late season; severe infection causes total crop failure
immediate_actions: Immediately apply systemic fungicide; alert neighboring farms; contact local agricultural extension; harvest early if grain fill is complete
preventive_measures: Resistant varieties with Sr31 and Sr38 genes; avoid late planting; destroy volunteer wheat
chemical_controls: Tebuconazole, Propiconazole, Mancozeb+Carbendazim combinations; apply before flag leaf emergence
organic_controls: Copper oxychloride; sulfur-based sprays (less effective for severe outbreaks)
seasonal_risk: Late spring to early summer; Ug99 strain is highly virulent worldwide
spread_mechanism: Wind-dispersed spores; can travel thousands of kilometers; requires alternate host barberry for sexual reproduction
notes: Ug99 race is a global biosecurity threat; notify authorities if suspected; most damaging rust disease of wheat

symptoms: Bright yellow-orange pustules arranged in stripes along leaf veins; leaves turn yellow then brown; young plants may die; flag leaf and ear infection causes severe yield loss
immediate_actions: Apply fungicide immediately; this disease progresses rapidly in cool weather; prioritize treatment of flag leaf and ear
preventive_measures: Resistant varieties with Yr genes; avoid dense planting; early sowing; monitor from tillering stage
chemical_controls: Propiconazole, Triadimefon, Tebuconazole, Boscalid+Epoxiconazole
organic_controls: Sulfur sprays; potassium silicate to strengthen cell walls
seasonal_risk: Autumn and spring; thrives at 7-15C; severe in cool wet seasons
spread_mechanism: Wind-dispersed spores; survives on green tissue year-round in mild climates
notes: Most destructive in cool highland regions; can cause 70-100% yield loss if untreated on susceptible varieties

symptoms: White fluffy mycelium on upper leaf surface; lesions turn grey-brown with black dots; stunted tillers; severe infection on flag leaf and ear reduces grain filling
immediate_actions: Improve airflow by thinning dense canopy; avoid excess nitrogen fertilizer; apply fungicide if more than 10% of leaves affected
preventive_measures: Resistant varieties; balanced fertilization; wider row spacing; avoid late sowing
chemical_controls: Fenpropimorph, Spiroxamine, Tebuconazole, Metrafenone; most effective at early growth stages
organic_controls: Potassium bicarbonate spray; baking soda at 1 tbsp per liter with dish soap; neem oil
seasonal_risk: Spring; high humidity with warm days and cool nights; dense crops with high nitrogen
spread_mechanism: Wind-dispersed conidia; does not need free water; multiplies rapidly in warm dry conditions
notes: Unlike most fungi powdery mildew infects in dry conditions; thrives with high nitrogen levels

symptoms: Water-soaked lesions on leaves turning dark brown with pale green border; white fuzzy sporulation on leaf underside; stems blacken; tubers show copper-brown rot; entire plant can collapse in 7-10 days
immediate_actions: Remove and bag all infected tissue; do not compost; apply copper fungicide or systemic; reduce irrigation; harvest tubers early if plants dying
preventive_measures: Certified disease-free seed; resistant varieties; avoid overhead irrigation; destroy cull piles; 3-4 week crop rotation
chemical_controls: Mancozeb+Metalaxyl (Ridomil Gold), Cymoxanil+Mancozeb (Curzate), Fluopicolide (Profiler), copper fungicides prophylactically
organic_controls: Copper hydroxide spray (Kocide); Bordeaux mixture; fixed copper
seasonal_risk: Wet cool conditions 10-24C; fog-prone areas; nights below 12C with dew
spread_mechanism: Sporangia spread by wind and water splash; zoospores released in wet conditions; survives in infected tubers
notes: Caused by Phytophthora infestans; responsible for Irish Potato Famine; one of most destructive plant diseases in history

symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings (target-board appearance) on older lower leaves; yellow halo around spots; defoliation from bottom up; stem lesions on seedlings; fruit lesions dark sunken with concentric rings
immediate_actions: Remove lower infected leaves; mulch soil to prevent spore splash; improve drainage; apply fungicide
preventive_measures: Crop rotation 3+ years; resistant varieties; stake plants for airflow; avoid wetting foliage; remove crop debris
chemical_controls: Chlorothalonil (Bravo), Mancozeb, Azoxystrobin, Difenoconazole; spray every 7-14 days
organic_controls: Copper oxychloride; neem oil; Bacillus subtilis (Serenade); compost tea; sulfur fungicide
seasonal_risk: Hot humid summer weather; stressed plants more susceptible; common late season
spread_mechanism: Conidia spread by wind and rain splash; survives on plant debris and soil
notes: Starts on older leaves and moves up; usually appears mid-season; distinct from late blight by target-ring pattern

affected_crops: Tomato, Pepper, Banana, Melon, Watermelon, Cotton
symptoms: Yellowing of lower leaves first moving upward; brown vascular discoloration when stem cut crosswise; plants wilt on hot afternoons then recover overnight initially; eventual complete plant death
immediate_actions: Remove and destroy infected plants; solarize soil; do not replant susceptible crops for 5+ years
preventive_measures: Resistant varieties; soil solarization; crop rotation with non-host; raised beds with good drainage; avoid wounding roots
chemical_controls: Seed treatment with Thiram or Captan; soil drench with Thiophanate-methyl; fumigation with Chloropicrin professional only
organic_controls: Trichoderma harzianum soil treatment; Bacillus subtilis; compost tea; mycorrhizal fungi inoculant
seasonal_risk: Warm dry seasons; survives indefinitely in soil as chlamydospores
spread_mechanism: Soil-borne; enters through roots; spreads via contaminated tools, boots, and irrigation water
notes: No fungicide completely effective once plant infected; prevention is the only reliable strategy; soil is permanently contaminated

symptoms: Rectangular lesions bounded by leaf veins; grey to tan color; lesions run parallel to veins; severe infection causes large areas of dead leaf tissue; stalk rots often follow
immediate_actions: Apply foliar fungicide at first sign; improve field drainage; avoid planting in low-lying areas
preventive_measures: Resistant hybrids; crop rotation with non-host; tillage to bury residue; adequate plant spacing for airflow
chemical_controls: Azoxystrobin (Amistar), Propiconazole (Tilt), Pyraclostrobin+Metconazole (Headline AMP)
organic_controls: Copper fungicides partially effective; biological fungicides with Bacillus subtilis
seasonal_risk: High humidity; warm nights above 15C; continuous corn fields with crop residue on surface
spread_mechanism: Asexual conidia spread by wind and rain; survives on corn residue; worst in minimum-till fields
notes: Major problem in humid subtropical corn regions; can reduce yield 50% or more in severe outbreaks

symptoms: Long cigar-shaped tan-gray lesions 2.5-15 cm long with wavy margins; appear first on lower leaves; severe cases affect entire plant causing premature death and stalk rots
immediate_actions: Apply fungicide at or before tasseling if disease present on third leaf from top; manage residue
preventive_measures: Resistant hybrids with Ht genes; crop rotation; bury residue with tillage; balanced fertilization
chemical_controls: Azoxystrobin, Propiconazole, Pyraclostrobin, Tebuconazole; most effective when applied at tassel emergence
organic_controls: Copper-based fungicides moderate efficacy
seasonal_risk: Cool wet conditions 18-27C; high humidity; dew periods
spread_mechanism: Wind-borne conidia; survives on corn residue; spreads rapidly in wet weather
notes: Reduces photosynthetic area during critical grain fill; stalk quality declines after severe infection

symptoms: Diamond or eye-shaped lesions on leaves with grey centre and brown border; neck rot causing white empty heads; collar rot on nodes; seedling blight; most severe disease of rice worldwide
immediate_actions: Apply fungicide immediately; avoid excess nitrogen; drain and dry fields periodically; report to extension office
preventive_measures: Resistant varieties; balanced nitrogen with split applications; early planting; dry direct-seeding when possible
chemical_controls: Tricyclazole (Bean), Isoprothiolane (Fuji-One), Azoxystrobin, Propiconazole; preventive application 10 days before heading critical
organic_controls: Silicon soil amendments increase blast resistance; neem cake application; Trichoderma harzianum soil treatment
seasonal_risk: High humidity and nitrogen; moderate temperatures 24-28C; blast explodes when nights cool and humid
spread_mechanism: Wind and rain-dispersed conidia; survives on straw and seeds; can spread between fields rapidly
notes: Most important rice disease globally; can destroy entire crop; panicle blast at heading is most destructive phase

symptoms: Water-soaked lesions on leaf margins turning yellow then white; dried lesions have wave-like margin; kresek stage causes entire young seedlings to wilt and die; creamy bacterial ooze on cut stem ends in water
immediate_actions: Drain flooded fields; reduce nitrogen fertilizer; remove and burn severely infected tillers
preventive_measures: Resistant varieties with Xa genes; reduce plant density; avoid excessive nitrogen; use clean seed; disinfect field equipment
chemical_controls: Copper hydroxide or Bordeaux mixture; no highly effective bactericide; copper compounds reduce spread
organic_controls: Garlic extract spray; copper sulfate diluted; no fully effective organic treatment
seasonal_risk: Humid tropical conditions; temperatures 25-34C; flooding promotes infection
spread_mechanism: Bacteria enter through water pores and wounds; spreads through floodwater and rain splash; persists in soil and weed hosts
notes: Caused by Xanthomonas oryzae; devastating in tropical Asia; once established in a field very hard to eliminate

symptoms: Tan-brown lesions with orange-rust pustule visible on leaf underside; upper surface shows small pale spots; severe defoliation; can cause 10-80% yield loss
immediate_actions: Apply fungicide at first sign; scout regularly from R1 flowering to R6 full seed
preventive_measures: Resistant varieties with Rpp genes where available; early planting to avoid late season peak; destroy volunteer soybean
chemical_controls: Azoxystrobin+Chlorothalonil, Tebuconazole, Pyraclostrobin+Fluxapyroxad (Priaxor); timing at R1-R3 critical
organic_controls: No fully effective organic options; copper fungicides provide partial protection
seasonal_risk: Late summer and autumn; warm humid conditions 15-28C
spread_mechanism: Wind-borne urediniospores; long-distance spread possible; requires living host tissue
notes: Caused by Phakopsora pachyrhizi; discovered in Americas in 2001; now present across all major soy-producing regions

symptoms: Upward curling of leaves; thickened veins; leaf enations (cup-like outgrowths) on leaf underside; stunted plant; reduced boll formation; can cause 80-100% yield loss in severe cases
immediate_actions: Remove and destroy infected plants; control whitefly vector immediately with insecticide; isolate infected fields
preventive_measures: Resistant or tolerant varieties; control whitefly Bemisia tabaci using yellow sticky traps and insecticides; remove weed hosts
chemical_controls: Imidacloprid or Thiamethoxam systemic insecticide for whitefly control; no cure for infected plants
organic_controls: Neem-based insecticides for whitefly; reflective mulches to repel vectors; yellow sticky traps
seasonal_risk: Hot dry conditions favor whitefly vector; epidemic seasons vary by region
spread_mechanism: Transmitted by whitefly Bemisia tabaci; virus not soil-borne; spreads as fast as whitefly moves
notes: Viral disease (Cotton leaf curl virus); management focuses on vector control not the virus itself

symptoms: Orange-yellow powdery pustules on leaf underside; upper surface shows pale yellow spots; defoliation; bare branches; yield reduction 30-80%
immediate_actions: Apply copper fungicide or triazole; remove severely infected branches; reduce shade to improve airflow; avoid overhead irrigation
preventive_measures: Shade management with proper spacing; resistant varieties such as Catimor and Timor hybrid; balanced nutrition; copper preventive sprays from flowering
chemical_controls: Copper hydroxide (Kocide), Mancozeb, Triadimefon, Tebuconazole; apply every 21 days during rainy season
organic_controls: Bordeaux mixture classical organic treatment; copper sulfate+lime; wettable sulfur
seasonal_risk: Rainy season with temperatures 21-25C; high humidity; dense shade canopy
spread_mechanism: Urediniospores spread by wind and rain; survives on living coffee leaves
notes: Caused by Hemileia vastatrix; devastated Sri Lanka coffee in 1870s; periodic epidemics in Central America

affected_crops: Grape, Cucumber, Squash, Melon, Basil, Onion
symptoms: Yellow oil spots on upper leaf surface; white-grey fuzzy sporulation on underside in humid conditions; brown necrotic lesions; shoot tips may die back in grapes; fruit infection common in cucurbits
immediate_actions: Apply fungicide preventively; remove infected tissue; reduce humidity; drip irrigation instead of overhead
preventive_measures: Resistant varieties; remove crop debris; improve drainage; wide spacing; avoid wetting foliage at night
chemical_controls: Metalaxyl+Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold), Cymoxanil, Dimethomorph (Forum), Fosetyl-aluminium (Aliette); alternate modes of action to prevent resistance
organic_controls: Bordeaux mixture; copper-based fungicides; Bacillus subtilis (Serenade); phosphonic acid (Agri-Fos) for grapes
seasonal_risk: Cool wet conditions 12-25C; free water on leaves required for infection; morning dew periods promote sporulation
spread_mechanism: Sporangia dispersed by wind and water splash; infected seeds carry pathogen; survives in soil for years
notes: Different pathogens cause downy mildew on different hosts; highly host-specific

symptoms: White powdery coating on shoots, leaves, tendrils and berries; berry cracking when infected early; corky patches on berries; reduced fruit quality and quantity; leaves may curl downward
immediate_actions: Apply sulfur or systemic fungicide; avoid excessive shading; improve airflow by canopy management
preventive_measures: Resistant varieties; open canopy via leaf removal; pruning for airflow; preventive spray program from bud break
chemical_controls: Myclobutanil (Eagle/Nova), Tebuconazole, Quinoxyfen (Elite), Spiroxamine; alternate fungicide classes to prevent resistance
organic_controls: Wettable sulfur most effective organic option; potassium bicarbonate; neem oil; jojoba oil
seasonal_risk: Warm dry conditions 17-28C; high humidity without rainfall; unlike most fungi does not need free water
spread_mechanism: Conidia dispersed by wind; mycelium overwinters in infected buds; can spread from single bud to entire vineyard
notes: Caused by Erysiphe necator; critical to protect flowers and young berries in first 3-4 weeks after bloom

affected_crops: Mango, Bean, Avocado, Papaya, Chili, Tomato, Banana
symptoms: Dark sunken lesions on fruit turning black; pink-salmon spore masses in moist conditions; leaf spots with yellow halo; twig dieback; blossom blight; circular depressed fruit lesions
immediate_actions: Remove all infected fruit and debris; apply copper or systemic fungicide; pick fruit at early maturity to reduce field infection
preventive_measures: Postharvest hot-water dip at 52C for 5 min for mango; orchard sanitation; resistant varieties; reduce canopy humidity
chemical_controls: Copper oxychloride, Mancozeb, Azoxystrobin, Difenoconazole; apply from flowering through harvest
organic_controls: Bordeaux mixture; copper hydroxide; biological control with Trichoderma; hot water treatment postharvest
seasonal_risk: Warm wet conditions 25-30C; infection occurs at flowering but symptoms appear postharvest
spread_mechanism: Rain-splash conidia; wind dispersal; survives on dead wood and infected tissue; latent infection common in mango
notes: Postharvest disease major problem in mangoes; infections are latent meaning invisible at harvest and appear during ripening

symptoms: Shoot tips wilt and curl into characteristic shepherds crook; flowers and young fruit turn black and shrivel but remain attached; bark water-soaked then sunken and reddish-brown; wood beneath bark shows orange-red discoloration
immediate_actions: Prune infected wood 30 cm below visible infection; sterilize tools between cuts with 10% bleach or 70% ethanol; do not work when wet
preventive_measures: Resistant varieties; avoid excess nitrogen; avoid overhead irrigation; copper spray at dormancy; biological sprays at bloom
chemical_controls: Streptomycin at bloom where legal; Kasugamycin; copper hydroxide at bud swell; Prohexadione-calcium to reduce shoot vigor
organic_controls: Serenade (Bacillus subtilis) biological control; BlightBan (Pseudomonas fluorescens); copper sprays at dormancy
seasonal_risk: Warm wet spring weather 24-29C during bloom; any wound or natural opening can be infected
spread_mechanism: Insect vectors bees and flies; rain and wind spread bacteria from cankers; bacterial ooze infects through flowers and wounds
notes: Bacterial disease caused by Erwinia amylovora; can kill entire orchard in 2 seasons; no cure once wood infected

affected_crops: Orange, Lemon, Grapefruit, Lime, all citrus species
symptoms: Raised corky tan-brown lesions with yellow halo on leaves, fruit, and twigs; lesions on fruit reduce marketability; defoliation in severe cases; premature fruit drop; water-soaked lesions initially
immediate_actions: Remove and destroy infected plant material; apply copper bactericide; notify authorities as this is a regulated pest in many countries
preventive_measures: Windbreaks to reduce leaf-to-leaf contact; resistant varieties; avoid movement of plant material; strict sanitation; copper sprays
chemical_controls: Copper hydroxide (Kocide), Copper oxychloride; no effective systemic bactericide; spray preventively before wet season
organic_controls: Copper bactericides; Bordeaux mixture; no fully effective organic cure once infected
seasonal_risk: Wet warm conditions 20-30C; wind-driven rain spreads disease; infection through wounds and natural openings
spread_mechanism: Bacteria spread by wind-driven rain, insects, contaminated tools; human movement of infected nursery stock causes long-distance spread
notes: Caused by Xanthomonas citri; quarantine disease in many countries; eradication campaigns conducted historically

symptoms: Yellowing of outer leaves starting from margin; leaves collapse at petiole; internal brown-red discoloration of pseudostem; plant collapse; Tropical Race 4 affects Cavendish the most commercial variety
immediate_actions: Remove and destroy affected plants; do not replant susceptible banana in same area for 10+ years; quarantine affected zones immediately; alert authorities
preventive_measures: Resistant varieties such as FHIA hybrids; strict biosecurity and disinfect boots, tools, vehicles; soil solarization; do not take cuttings from affected areas
chemical_controls: No fungicide effective; Thiabendazole soil drench provides minimal protection; fumigation with Dazomet occasionally used
organic_controls: Trichoderma and Bacillus subtilis as preventive biocontrol before infection; silicon nutrition to strengthen plants
seasonal_risk: Any time of year; Fusarium oxysporum f.sp. cubense persists indefinitely in soil
spread_mechanism: Soil-borne fungus; spreads via infected planting material, contaminated soil, water; TR4 spreads 1-10 km per year through water
notes: TR4 race is a global threat to Cavendish banana production; declared by FAO as major threat to global food security

affected_crops: Strawberry, Grape, Tomato, Lettuce, Cucumber, Raspberry
symptoms: Grey fuzzy sporulation on infected tissue; water-soaked lesions becoming covered in grey spore masses; fruit turns to mush; stem lesions cause wilt above the infection
immediate_actions: Remove infected fruit and plant parts immediately; improve airflow; reduce humidity; avoid wetting foliage; pick fruit more frequently
preventive_measures: Wide plant spacing; stake plants; avoid wounding; remove senescing flowers; plastic mulch; reduce canopy density
chemical_controls: Iprodione (Rovral), Fenhexamid (Decree), Boscalid, Cyprodinil+Fludioxonil (Switch); resistance common so rotate modes of action
organic_controls: Trichoderma harzianum; Bacillus subtilis (Serenade); potassium bicarbonate; neem oil; cinnamon oil
seasonal_risk: Cool wet conditions 15-20C; high humidity above 93%; poor airflow; damage or senescence promotes infection
spread_mechanism: Airborne conidia in billions per plant; wounds and senescing tissue are entry points; survives as sclerotia in soil
notes: Caused by Botrytis cinerea; ubiquitous saprophyte that turns pathogenic under stress; major postharvest disease of berries

affected_crops: Soybean, Sunflower, Canola, Bean, Carrot, Lettuce
symptoms: White cottony mycelium on stems; water-soaked lesions; stems bleach and shred; hard black sclerotia 1-20mm inside and outside stems; crop lodging; hollow pith in stems
immediate_actions: Remove infected plants; avoid overhead irrigation; improve field drainage; reduce plant density
preventive_measures: Resistant varieties; crop rotation 3+ years; deep tillage to bury sclerotia; control canopy density
chemical_controls: Thiophanate-methyl, Carbendazim, Boscalid (Endura), Fluazinam (Omega); apply at early flowering
organic_controls: Coniothyrium minitans (Contans WG) is biological control agent specific to Sclerotinia sclerotia; apply to soil
seasonal_risk: Cool moist conditions 15-22C; dense canopy trapping humidity; infection occurs through dead flower petals
spread_mechanism: Airborne ascospores from soil-level apothecia; sclerotia survive in soil 5-10 years; spreads in contaminated soil and seed
notes: Caused by Sclerotinia sclerotiorum; very wide host range; sclerotia persist indefinitely in soil making eradication impossible

affected_crops: Common bean, Cowpea, Lima bean, Cucumber, Cotton
symptoms: Angular water-soaked lesions bounded by veins turning brown; white bacterial exudate in humid weather; brown lesions with yellow halo; seed discoloration and shriveling; severe defoliation
immediate_actions: Remove infected leaves; reduce overhead irrigation; avoid field work when plants are wet
preventive_measures: Certified disease-free seed; crop rotation 2+ years; resistant varieties; copper spray preventively; avoid wounding plants
chemical_controls: Copper hydroxide, Copper oxychloride; bactericides copper-based only; no effective systemic bactericide
organic_controls: Copper-based sprays; Bordeaux mixture
seasonal_risk: Wet warm conditions 24-32C; rain splash critical for spread
spread_mechanism: Rain splash and irrigation water; infected seed; contaminated tools; human movement through wet crops
notes: Caused by Pseudomonas syringae or Xanthomonas spp. depending on host; seed transmission makes clean seed programs essential

symptoms: Small water-soaked spots turning dark brown with yellow halo on leaves; raised scabby lesions on fruit reducing marketability; severe defoliation; spots may have greasy appearance initially
immediate_actions: Reduce overhead irrigation; apply copper bactericide; remove heavily infected plants in severe outbreaks
preventive_measures: Copper spray preventive program; resistant varieties; certified seed; crop rotation 2+ years; drip irrigation
chemical_controls: Copper hydroxide+Mancozeb; Copper oxychloride; Kasugamycin; preventive application critical as curative effect is minimal
organic_controls: Fixed copper; Bordeaux mixture; neem oil has some bacteriostatic effect
seasonal_risk: Warm wet conditions 24-30C; rain and high humidity; overhead irrigation promotes spread
spread_mechanism: Rain splash; wind; contaminated tools and clothing; seed-borne; hands when handling wet plants
notes: Caused by Xanthomonas vesicatoria complex; strains with copper resistance becoming widespread; application timing critical

symptoms: Large galls 1-15 cm on ears, tassels, stalks, and leaves filled with black powdery spores; galls initially white-grey covered by thin membrane; infected plants may be stunted
immediate_actions: Remove and destroy galls before they rupture and release spores; bag galls before removal; do not compost
preventive_measures: Avoid wounding plants from insects, hail, and cultivator damage; avoid dense planting; resistant varieties; crop rotation
chemical_controls: No effective fungicide once infection established; seed treatments with Thiram or Metalaxyl reduce early infection
organic_controls: No effective organic treatment for established infection; prevention through crop hygiene only
seasonal_risk: Hot dry periods followed by rain; temperatures 26-34C; wounding events such as hail and insects precede infection
spread_mechanism: Teliospores soil-borne and airborne; enter through wounds and growing points; not spread plant-to-plant during season
notes: Caused by Ustilago maydis; considered delicacy called huitlacoche in Mexico; galls should be harvested young at white stage if using as food

symptoms: Tan to brown irregular blotches on leaves with yellow borders; small black pycnidia (visible with hand lens) scattered within lesions; lesions start on lower leaves and move upward; flag leaf infection dramatically reduces yield
immediate_actions: Apply triazole or strobilurin fungicide at first sign on flag leaf; improve field drainage; avoid overhead irrigation
preventive_measures: Resistant varieties; crop rotation; deep tillage to bury infected residue; delayed sowing; balanced nitrogen
chemical_controls: Tebuconazole, Propiconazole, Azoxystrobin, Epoxiconazole; timing critical between GS31 and GS39 (stem extension to flag leaf)
organic_controls: Copper fungicides partial protection; Bacillus subtilis biological control
seasonal_risk: Wet cool conditions 15-22C; rain splash essential for spread; most damaging in high rainfall areas
spread_mechanism: Rain splash dispersal of pycnidiospores; also airborne ascospores from sexual stage; survives on stubble
notes: Most economically important foliar disease of wheat in temperate regions; fungicide resistance emerging to triazoles and strobilurins

symptoms: Pink-orange sporulation on bleached spikelets called tombstone kernels; partial or complete bleaching of heads; severely infected grain shriveled and lightweight; mycotoxin contamination (DON/vomitoxin and zearalenone) makes grain unmarketable or unsafe
immediate_actions: Apply fungicide at early flowering; harvest as soon as possible; dry grain quickly to below 14% moisture; test grain for mycotoxins before use
preventive_measures: Resistant varieties; crop rotation away from corn and small grains; deep tillage; avoid late flowering during wet weather
chemical_controls: Tebuconazole, Metconazole (Caramba), Prothioconazole (Proline); apply at 50% anthesis (Zadoks 65) for best efficacy
organic_controls: Trichoderma and Bacillus subtilis biological controls as seed treatment
seasonal_risk: Warm wet conditions 25-30C during flowering; rain events at anthesis critical for infection
spread_mechanism: Airborne ascospores from corn and wheat residue; macroconidia splash dispersed; infected seed
notes: Mycotoxin contamination is public health concern; DON causes vomiting in pigs and feed refusal; zearalenone causes reproductive failure

symptoms: Uniform green coloration appropriate for crop and growth stage; no spots, lesions, pustules, or discoloration; normal leaf texture and turgor; appropriate growth for the season and variety
immediate_actions: No treatment needed; continue regular monitoring and good agronomic practices
preventive_measures: Continue preventive practices including balanced fertilization, proper irrigation, crop rotation, weed management, and regular weekly scouting during growing season
chemical_controls: No pesticide application needed
organic_controls: Continue good soil health practices including compost, cover crops, and beneficial insect habitat
seasonal_risk: All seasons; healthy plants are better able to resist disease
spread_mechanism: Not applicable
notes: Regular monitoring is the key to early disease detection; a healthy crop scan is a good outcome; maintain records of scan dates and locations for trend analysis
